title: Analytically weak and mild solutions to stochastic heat equation with irregular drift
overall_summary: The paper studies the stochastic heat equation ‚ąāt u = 1/2 ‚ąāxx u + b(u) + space-time white noise when the scalar drift b is a generalized function in a Besov/H√∂lder-Zygmund space, on D=R or on [0,1] with periodic or Neumann boundary conditions. Its main result is an equivalence theorem: in the regularity regime őĪ > -3/2, weak regularized solutions and mild regularized solutions coincide for processes in the class V(5/8), and for b in Lp all standard analytically weak, analytically mild, weak regularized, and mild regularized notions are equivalent. The core technique is stochastic sewing with random controls, used to obtain robust occupation-type bounds for integrals of singular functions of V+Ōą, where V is the stochastic convolution and Ōą is a controlled perturbation. The paper is useful for the original invariant-measure problem mainly because it justifies passing between weak and mild formulations and between distributional drifts and smooth drift approximations, assuming the same theory extends to Dirichlet boundary conditions. It does not prove invariant-measure uniqueness or ergodicity, but it supplies the solution-concept and approximation infrastructure needed before such arguments can be made rigorous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Convention 2.1 and stochastic convolution setup
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper fixes one of three spatial settings. Either D=R with heat kernel g_t(x-y) and test functions S(D) equal to the Schwartz space, or D=[0,1] with periodic boundary conditions, periodic heat kernel p_t^per, and smooth periodic test functions, or D=[0,1] with Neumann boundary conditions, Neumann heat kernel p_t^Neu, and smooth test functions satisfying Neumann boundary conditions. The corresponding heat semigroup is denoted P. If W is space-time white noise on [0,T]√óD, the stochastic convolution is V_t(x)=‚ąę_0^t ‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y) W(dr,dy), for t‚Č•0 and x‚ąąD. The scalar drift b is a distribution in a Besov/H√∂lder-Zygmund space; C^őĪ denotes B^őĪ_{‚ąě,‚ąě}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is notation and setup rather than a result. The three kernels are explicitly defined or named, and all later definitions and estimates are stated uniformly for the triple (D,p,S(D)). The paper does not include Dirichlet boundary conditions in its formal statements.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 2.2 and 2.5: analytically mild and analytically weak solutions for measurable drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings D‚ąą{R, periodic [0,1], Neumann [0,1]}, let P be the corresponding heat semigroup, and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let b:R‚ÜíR be measurable and u0‚ąąB(D), the bounded measurable functions on D. A jointly continuous adapted process u:(0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR is an analytically mild solution if, for each t‚ąą[0,T] and x‚ąąD, ‚ąę_0^t ‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))| dy dr <‚ąě almost surely and u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+‚ąę_0^t ‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b(u_r(y)) dy dr+V_t(x) almost surely. A jointly continuous adapted process u is an analytically weak solution if, for every test function Ōē‚ąąS(D), almost surely ‚ü®u_t,Ōē‚ü©=‚ü®u0,Ōē‚ü©+‚ąę_0^t ‚ü®u_s,1/2 őĒŌē‚ü© ds+‚ąę_0^t ‚ü®b(u_s),Ōē‚ü© ds+W_t(Ōē), for all t‚ąą[0,T], with all integrals well defined.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions. The paper notes that for unbounded b, the full-probability set in the analytically mild formulation may depend on t and x, following the classical random-field definition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.4: mild regularized solution for distributional drift
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three heat-kernel settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let ő≤‚ąąR, q‚ąą[1,‚ąě], b‚ąąB^ő≤_q(R), and u0‚ąąB(D). A jointly continuous adapted process u:(0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR is a mild regularized solution of ‚ąāt u=1/2 őĒu+b(u)+white noise if there exists a jointly continuous process K:[0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR such that: (1) almost surely u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+K_t(x)+V_t(x) for all x‚ąąD and t‚ąą(0,T]; and (2) for every sequence b_n‚ąąC‚ąě_b(R) such that b_n‚Üíb in B^{ő≤‚ąí}_q, meaning sup_n ||b_n||_{B^ő≤_q}<‚ąě and ||b_n-b||_{B^{ő≤‚Ä≤}_q}‚Üí0 for every ő≤‚Ä≤<ő≤, one has, for every N>0, sup_{t‚ąą[0,T]} sup_{x‚ąąD, |x|‚Č§N} |‚ąę_0^t ‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b_n(u_r(y)) dy dr - K_t(x)| ‚Üí0 in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition. The paper observes that every Besov distribution f can be approximated in this sense by smooth bounded functions, for example by heat-kernel mollification G_{1/n}f. When b is sufficiently regular and b_n converges uniformly to b, the definition reduces to the usual analytically mild formulation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.6: weak regularized solution for distributional drift
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let S(D) be the corresponding test-function space. Let ő≤‚ąąR, q‚ąą[1,‚ąě], b‚ąąB^ő≤_q(R), and u0‚ąąB(D). A jointly continuous adapted process u:(0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR is a weak regularized solution if there exists a continuous-in-time process K:[0,T]√óS(D)√óő©‚ÜíR such that: (1) for every Ōē‚ąąS(D), almost surely ‚ü®u_t,Ōē‚ü©=‚ü®u0,Ōē‚ü©+‚ąę_0^t ‚ü®u_s,1/2 őĒŌē‚ü© ds+K_t(Ōē)+W_t(Ōē), for all t‚ąą[0,T]; and (2) for every b_n‚ąąC‚ąě_b(R) with b_n‚Üíb in B^{ő≤‚ąí}_q and every Ōē‚ąąS(D), sup_{t‚ąą[0,T]} |‚ąę_0^t ‚ąę_D Ōē(x)b_n(u_r(x)) dx dr - K_t(Ōē)| ‚Üí0 in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition. The paper notes that if ő≤>0, one may choose b_n converging uniformly to b, and the weak regularized notion agrees with the usual analytically weak solution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.8: the regularity class V(őļ)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. For őļ‚ąą[0,1], an adapted measurable process u:[0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR belongs to V(őļ) if for every m‚Č•2, sup_{(t,x)‚ąą(0,T]√óD} ||u_t(x)||_{L^m(ő©)}<‚ąě and sup_{0<s‚Č§t‚Č§T} sup_{x‚ąąD} ||u_t(x)-V_t(x)-P_{t-s}[u_s-V_s](x)||_{L^m(ő©)} / |t-s|^őļ <‚ąě. If u=P_tu0+K_t+V_t is a mild regularized solution, the numerator is exactly ||K_t(x)-P_{t-s}K_s(x)||_{L^m}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition. Its role is to quantify heat-semigroup time regularity of the drift component of u after subtracting the stochastic convolution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10: equivalence of weak and mild regularized solutions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings D=R, periodic [0,1], or Neumann [0,1]. Let őĪ>-3/2, b‚ąąC^őĪ(R), u0‚ąąB(D), and let u:[0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be a measurable adapted process. Assume u‚ąąV(5/8). Then u is a weak regularized solution of ‚ąāt u=1/2 őĒu+b(u)+white noise with initial condition u0 if and only if u is a mild regularized solution of the same equation with initial condition u0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is split into two directions. For weak regularized implies mild regularized, the paper first proves Lemma 4.1: for any controlled perturbation u‚ąąV(ŌĄ) and f‚ąąC^ő≥ with ő≥+4ŌĄ>1, the smoothed drift integrals against smooth approximations f_n converge to canonical limiting processes in both mild and weak forms. Applying this to f=b produces a candidate mild drift H^b. Lemmas 4.3 and 4.4 convert an abstract weak identity with a continuous distributional drift term K(Ōē) into a mild-type identity u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+R^K(t,x)+V_t(x), where R^K is obtained as the limit of ‚ąę_0^{t-őĶ} K(dr,p_{t-r}(x,¬∑)). Lemma 4.5 then identifies R^K with the same limit H^b by comparing ‚ąę_0^t p_{t-r}b_n(u_r) with R^K, using time discretization, the weak regularized approximation property, and stochastic sewing estimates. Since both processes are continuous, equality on rational times and positions extends everywhere. For mild regularized implies weak regularized, Lemma 4.8 starts from u=P_tu0+K_t+V_t and uses a telescoping sum over the time grid i/n to show that for each test function Ōē there is a continuous process R_Ōē satisfying the weak identity and represented as the limit of sums ‚ü®K_{i/n}-P_{1/n}K_{(i-1)/n},Ōē‚ü©. Lemma 4.9 then identifies R_Ōē as the limit of ‚ąęŌē b_n(u) for every approximating sequence b_n‚Üíb in C^{őĪ‚ąí}, again by time discretization and Proposition 3.4. This proves the two defining conditions of weak regularized solutions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.11: equivalence of four solution notions for Lp drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let p‚Č•1, b‚ąąL^p(R), u0‚ąąB(D), and let u:[0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be a measurable adapted process. Then the following are equivalent: (a) u is an analytically weak solution; (b) u is a weak regularized solution in V(5/8); (c) u is an analytically mild solution; and (d) u is a mild regularized solution in V(5/8). Moreover, if any of these conditions holds, then u‚ąąV(1-1/(4p)), with the natural convention that 1/(4p)=0 when p=‚ąě.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses Theorem 2.10 to identify weak and mild regularized solutions. The main additional step is to prove that any classical analytically weak or mild solution automatically lies in V(1-1/(4p)). For mild-type identities, this is Lemma 4.10, whose proof uses Lemma 4.11: applying the stochastic sewing bound of Lemma 3.3 to f=|b|, Ōą=u‚ąíV, and the random control w_{s,t}(x)=‚ąę_s^t‚ąęp_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr yields an L^m bound of order |t-s|^{1-1/(4p)} for the drift increment; continuity and Fatou extend the estimate from almost every x to every x. For analytically weak solutions, Lemma 4.12 and Corollary 4.13 first show that the mild formulation holds for Lebesgue-a.e. x, after testing against time-dependent heat-evolved test functions and passing to heat-kernel approximations. Once u‚ąąV(5/8), Corollary 3.5 shows that approximating b by smooth bounded b_n in the appropriate negative Besov topology yields convergence of the drift integrals in L^2, so the regularized and classical drift terms coincide. The reverse implications use the same Corollary 3.5 with b_n=G_{1/n}b to identify the regularized drift K with the actual Lebesgue integral involving b.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.12: existence and uniqueness for Lp drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let p‚Č•1, b‚ąąL^p(R), and u0‚ąąB(D). Then the stochastic heat equation ‚ąāt u=1/2 őĒu+b(u)+white noise has a unique analytically mild solution and a unique analytically weak solution, and these two solutions coincide. The solutions are strong in the probabilistic sense.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes the earlier existence and uniqueness theorem for mild regularized solutions from Athreya-Butkovsky-L√™-Mytnik, cited as [1, Theorem 2.6], which gives a strong probabilistic mild regularized solution and uniqueness in the class V(3/4). By Theorem 2.11, that solution is both analytically mild and analytically weak. Conversely, any analytically mild or analytically weak solution is a mild regularized solution and belongs to V(1-1/(4p))‚äāV(3/4), so the cited uniqueness theorem forces all such solutions to coincide.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2: stochastic sewing lemma with random controls
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let m‚ąą[2,‚ąě). Let őĽ be a random control on the simplex őĒ_{[0,T]}, meaning őĽ(s,u)+őĽ(u,t)‚Č§őĽ(s,t) almost surely whenever 0‚Č§s‚Č§u‚Č§t‚Č§T. Let A_{s,t} be a two-parameter adapted family and write őīA_{s,u,t}=A_{s,t}-A_{s,u}-A_{u,t}. Suppose there are constants őď1,őď2,őĪ1,őĪ2,ő≤2‚Č•0 with őĪ1>1/2 and őĪ2+ő≤2>1 such that, for every (s,t) and midpoint u=(s+t)/2, ||őīA_{s,u,t}||_{L^m}‚Č§őď1|t-s|^{őĪ1} and |E[őīA_{s,u,t} | F_u]|‚Č§őď2|t-s|^{őĪ2}őĽ(s,t)^{ő≤2} almost surely. Suppose also that there is a process A_t such that for every t and every sequence of partitions of [0,t] with mesh tending to zero, A_t is the in-probability limit of the Riemann sums ‚ąĎ A_{t_i,t_{i+1}}. Then there exists a measurable B_{s,t} and a constant C depending only on őĪ1,őĪ2,ő≤2,m such that |A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}|‚Č§Cőď2|t-s|^{őĪ2}őĽ(s,t)^{ő≤2}+B_{s,t} almost surely and ||B_{s,t}||_{L^m}‚Č§Cőď1|t-s|^{őĪ1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This result is quoted from the earlier ABLM paper as [1, Theorem 4.7]. In the present paper it is used as a black box. The key novelty relative to deterministic sewing is that the conditional expectation error is controlled by a random superadditive quantity őĽ, while the martingale part is controlled in L^m; this allows one to sew nonlinear drift integrals even when the perturbation Ōą has only random pathwise control.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3: integral bound with a random control
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let ő≥‚ąą(-2,0), T‚ąą(0,1], and let f:R‚ÜíR be measurable, either bounded or nonnegative. Let Ōą:[0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be adapted and measurable. Assume there is a nonnegative process w_{s,t}(x) on the time simplex and space such that for every t there is D_t‚äāD of full Lebesgue measure with, for all (s,u)‚ąąőĒ_{[0,t]} and x‚ąąD_t, |Ōą_t(x)-P_{t-s}Ōą_s(x)|‚Č§w_{s,t}(x) almost surely and P_{t-u}w_{s,u}(x)+w_{u,t}(x)‚Č§w_{s,t}(x) almost surely. Then there exists a nonnegative process L_{s,t}(x) such that for all (s,t)‚ąąőĒ_{[0,T]} and x‚ąąD_T, |‚ąę_s^t ‚ąę_D p_{T-r}(x,y) f(V_r(y)+Ōą_r(y)) dy dr| ‚Č§ C||f||_{C^ő≥}(t-s)^{(3+ő≥)/4} P_{T-t}w_{s,t}(x)+L_{s,t}(x), where C depends only on ő≥. Moreover, for every m‚ąą[2,‚ąě), ||L_{s,t}(x)||_{L^m}‚Č§C||f||_{C^ő≥}|t-s|^{1+ő≥/4}, with C depending only on ő≥ and m.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For f‚ąąC^1, the proof applies Proposition 3.2. Define A_{s,t}(x)=‚ąę_s^t‚ąęp_{T-r}(x,y) f(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}Ōą_s(y))dy dr and A_t(x)=‚ąę_0^t‚ąęp_{T-r}(x,y) f(V_r(y)+Ōą_r(y))dy dr. The random control is őĽ_{s,t}(x)=P_{T-t}w_{s,t}(x); the assumed inequality on w implies őĽ is a random control. For u=(s+t)/2, őīA_{s,u,t} is the integral over [u,t] of the difference between f evaluated at V_r+P_{r-s}Ōą_s and at V_r+P_{r-u}Ōą_u. Conditional expectation at time u is estimated using Gaussian smoothing of the future stochastic convolution: the derivative norm of the heat-smoothed f is bounded by C||f||_{C^ő≥}(r-u)^{(ő≥-1)/4}, and the difference of the deterministic shifts is bounded by P_{r-u}w_{s,u}. This gives the conditional sewing estimate with exponent (3+ő≥)/4 and őĽ. The L^m sewing estimate follows from an existing integral estimate for V plus a bounded perturbation, after truncating Ōą and passing to the limit. The reconstruction condition is verified by comparing A_t with Riemann sums and using Lipschitz continuity of f together with the random control. After sewing, f‚ąąC^1 is extended to bounded continuous f by heat mollification, to indicators of open sets by Urysohn's lemma and the embedding L^1‚äāC^{-1}, to bounded measurable f by Lusin approximation, and to nonnegative measurable f by truncation and Fatou's lemma.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4: deterministic L1-kernel integral bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let ŌĄ‚ąą(0,1], őĹ‚ąą[0,1/2), 0<T‚Č§1, ő≥‚ąą(-2,0), and őĒ>0. Let f:R‚ÜíR be measurable, either bounded or nonnegative, let X:[0,T)√óD‚ÜíR be measurable, and let Ōą:[0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be adapted and measurable. Assume ő≥+4ŌĄ>1 and ‚ąę_D |X_t(y)|dy‚Č§őĒ(T-t)^{-őĹ} for t‚ąą[0,T). Then, for every m‚Č•2, there is C=C(ő≥,őĹ,ŌĄ,m) such that for every (s,t)‚ąąőĒ_{[0,T]}, ||‚ąę_s^t‚ąę_D X_r(y) f(V_r(y)+Ōą_r(y))dy dr||_{L^m} ‚Č§ C||f||_{C^ő≥}őĒ(t-s)^{1+ő≥/4-őĹ}(1+[Ōą]_{C^ŌĄ L^m([s,t])}(t-s)^{ŌĄ-1/4}), where [Ōą]_{C^ŌĄ L^m([s,t])}=sup_{a‚Č§b in [s,t]} sup_x ||Ōą_b(x)-P_{b-a}Ōą_a(x)||_{L^m}/|b-a|^ŌĄ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For continuous f this is quoted from [1, Lemma 6.3]. The present paper extends it to bounded measurable or nonnegative f by the same approximation procedure used in Lemma 3.3: heat mollify bounded continuous approximants, use Lusin approximation for bounded measurable functions, use the embedding of L^1 into negative H√∂lder/Besov spaces to control exceptional sets, and use truncation plus Fatou for nonnegative f. The estimate exploits the smoothing and decorrelation supplied by the stochastic convolution V and the assumption that Ōą is controlled in the semigroup H√∂lder norm.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5: Lp approximation bound for drift differences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let ő≥‚ąą(-3/2,-1), p‚ąą[1,‚ąě], let g,h:R‚ÜíR be measurable with g bounded and h‚ąąL^p(R), let X:[0,1]√óD‚ÜíR be measurable, and let u:[0,1]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be adapted and measurable. Then there is C=C(ő≥,p) such that for every t‚ąą[0,1], ||‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D X_r(y)(h-g)(u_r(y))dy dr||_{L^2} ‚Č§ C||h-g||_{C^ő≥}(1+[u‚ąíV]_{C^{5/8}L^2([0,1])}) sup_{r‚ąą[0,1]} ||X_r||_{L^1(D)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix N and split h‚ąíg=(h1_{|h|‚Č§N}‚ąíg)+h1_{|h|>N}. Apply Proposition 3.4 with ŌĄ=5/8, őĹ=0, T=1, Ōą=u‚ąíV, and f equal to the bounded first term, then apply it again to the nonnegative tail |h|1_{|h|>N}. Since ő≥<-1, L^p(R) embeds into C^ő≥, so the tail C^ő≥ norm is controlled by the L^p tail norm. Let N‚Üí‚ąě to remove the tail, using h‚ąąL^p. The result is uniform in X through only sup_r||X_r||_{L^1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6: Kolmogorov criterion for convergence of continuous processes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let ő≥1,ő≥2>0. Let X_n:[0,1]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be jointly continuous processes with X_n(0,x)=0. Suppose that for every m‚Č•1 there is C=C(m) such that, for all s,t‚ąą[0,1], x,y‚ąąD, and n, ||X_n(t,x)-X_n(s,y)||_{L^m}‚Č§C|t-s|^{ő≥1}+C|x-y|^{ő≥2}; and suppose lim_{n,k‚Üí‚ąě} sup_{t‚ąą[0,1]} sup_{x‚ąąD} ||X_n(t,x)-X_k(t,x)||_{L^m}=0. Then there is a jointly continuous process X such that for every N>0, sup_{s,t‚ąą[0,1]} sup_{x‚ąąD, |x|‚Č§N} |X(t,x)-X_n(t,x)|‚Üí0 in probability. Moreover, for every ŌĀ‚ąą(0,1) and őĽ>ő≥2‚ąíő≥2ŌĀ, sup_{s,t‚ąą[0,1]} sup_{x,y‚ąąD} |X(t,x)-X(s,y)| / ((|t-s|^{ő≥1}+|x-y|^{ő≥2})^ŌĀ(1+|x|+|y|)^őĽ) <‚ąě almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof applies a Kolmogorov continuity theorem with spatial weights, cited as [3, Proposition C.1], to obtain a jointly continuous limit and the weighted H√∂lder bound. Fatou's lemma transfers the moment increment estimates from X_n to the limiting process X. Local uniform convergence in probability follows from the assumed uniform L^m Cauchy property plus the common Kolmogorov modulus.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1: canonical limits of regularized drift integrals for u‚ąąV(ŌĄ)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let ő≥‚ąą(-2,0), ŌĄ‚ąą(0,1), f‚ąąC^ő≥(R), and let u:(0,1]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be a jointly continuous adapted process with u‚ąąV(ŌĄ). Assume ő≥+4ŌĄ>1. Then: (i) there exists a jointly continuous adapted process H^f:[0,1]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR such that for every sequence f_n‚ąąC‚ąě_b(R) with f_n‚Üíf in C^{ő≥‚ąí} and every N>0, sup_{t‚ąą[0,1]} sup_{x‚ąąD, |x|‚Č§N} |‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)f_n(u_r(y))dy dr - H^f_t(x)| ‚Üí0 in probability. In addition, for every őĶ>0, sup_{s,t‚ąą[0,1]} sup_{x,y‚ąąD} |H^f_t(x)-H^f_s(y)| / ((|t-s|^{1/2‚ąíőĶ}+|x-y|^{1‚ąíőĶ})(1+|x|+|y|)^{2őĶ}) <‚ąě almost surely. (ii) For every Ōē‚ąąS(D), there exists a continuous process H^f(Ōē):[0,1]√óő©‚ÜíR such that for every f_n‚ąąC‚ąě_b with f_n‚Üíf in C^{ő≥‚ąí}, sup_{t‚ąą[0,1]} |‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D Ōē(x)f_n(u_r(x))dx dr - H^f_t(Ōē)| ‚Üí0 in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the mild limit, set H^{f,n}_t(x)=‚ąę_0^t‚ąęp_{t-r}(x,z)f_n(u_r(z))dz dr. Proposition 3.4 is applied with Ōą=u‚ąíV. Choosing ő≥‚Ä≤<ő≥ with ő≥‚Ä≤+4ŌĄ>1 gives uniform L^m H√∂lder bounds for H^{f,n}_t(x) in t and x: the time-increment term uses X_r=p_{t-r}(x,¬∑), and the spatial/time kernel-difference term uses X_r=p_{t-r}(x,¬∑)‚ąíp_{s-r}(y,¬∑), whose L^1 norm is bounded by heat-kernel estimates. Proposition 3.4 also gives uniform L^m Cauchy convergence because ||f_n‚ąíf_k||_{C^{ő≥‚Ä≤}}‚Üí0. Proposition 3.6 then yields a continuous limit H^f and local uniform convergence in probability, together with the weighted H√∂lder estimate. Independence of the approximating sequence is shown by interlacing two approximating sequences. The weak/test-function part is analogous, with H^{f,n}_t=‚ąę_0^t‚ąęŌē f_n(u), using Proposition 3.4 with X=Ōē and again applying the one-dimensional version of the convergence criterion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.2: spatial growth and H√∂lder regularity of the drift component
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let őĪ>-3/2, b‚ąąC^őĪ(R), u0‚ąąB(D), and suppose u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+K_t(x)+V_t(x) is a mild regularized solution of the equation with drift b and u‚ąąV(5/8). Then for every őĶ>0, sup_{s,t‚ąą[0,1]} sup_{x,y‚ąąD} |K_t(x)-K_s(y)| / ((|t-s|^{1/2‚ąíőĶ}+|x-y|^{1‚ąíőĶ})(1+|x|+|y|)^{2őĶ}) <‚ąě almost surely. Also, for every őĶ>0, sup_{t‚ąą[0,1]} sup_{x‚ąąD} |u_t(x)|/(1+|x|^őĶ)<‚ąě almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The mild regularized definition identifies K with the canonical limit H^b from Lemma 4.1, so the weighted H√∂lder bound for K follows from the H√∂lder conclusion there, using an admissible negative regularity below őĪ when needed. For V, standard moment estimates for the stochastic convolution give ||V(t,x)-V(s,y)||_{L^m}‚Č§C|t-s|^{1/4}+C|x-y|^{1/2}; Kolmogorov's theorem yields subpolynomial weighted growth. Since P_tu0 is uniformly bounded for bounded u0, the same subpolynomial growth holds for u=P_tu0+K+V.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3: time-dependent test functions for weak-type identities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let u0‚ąąB(D), let u:(0,1]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be jointly continuous, and let H:[0,1]√óS(D)√óő©‚ÜíR be continuous in time. Assume that for every Ōē‚ąąS(D), almost surely ‚ü®u_t,Ōē‚ü©=‚ü®u0,Ōē‚ü©+‚ąę_0^t ‚ü®u_s,1/2 őĒŌē‚ü©ds+H_t(Ōē)+W_t(Ōē), for all t‚ąą[0,1], with finite integrals. Then: (i) there is a full-probability set on which, for each ŌČ, there are m(ŌČ)‚ąąZ_+ and őď(ŌČ)>0 such that sup_{t‚ąą[0,1]} |‚ü®u_t(ŌČ),Ōē‚ü©|‚Č§őď(ŌČ)||Ōē||_{S(D),m(ŌČ)} for all Ōē‚ąąS(D), where ||¬∑||_{S(D),m} is the Hilbertian Schwartz-type seminorm defined in the paper; (ii) for every t‚ąą(0,1] and x‚ąąD, P_őĶu_{t-őĶ}(x)‚Üíu_t(x) almost surely as őĶ‚Üí0; (iii) for every f‚ąąC([0,1],S(D)), there is a continuous adapted process denoted ‚ąę_0^. H(ds,f_s) such that the Riemann sums ‚ąĎ_{i=1}^{‚Ćänt‚Ćč}(H_{i/n}(f_{(i-1)/n})‚ąíH_{(i-1)/n}(f_{(i-1)/n})) converge in probability to ‚ąę_0^t H(ds,f_s), and almost surely ‚ü®u_t,f_t‚ü©=‚ü®u0,f0‚ü©+‚ąę_0^t ‚ü®u_s,‚ąā_s f_s+1/2 őĒf_s‚ü©ds+‚ąę_0^t H(ds,f_s)+‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D f_s(x)W(ds,dx).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Part (i) uses the assumed weak identity to see that t‚Ü¶‚ü®u_t,Ōē‚ü© is almost surely continuous for each Ōē, then applies Mitoma's theorem for S(D)-valued distributions to obtain a random Schwartz-seminorm bound uniformly in time. For (ii), decompose P_őĶu_{t-őĶ}(x)=‚ü®u_{t-őĶ},p_őĶ(x,¬∑)Ōá(x‚ąí¬∑)‚ü©+‚ü®u_{t-őĶ},p_őĶ(x,¬∑)(1‚ąíŌá(x‚ąí¬∑))‚ü©, with Ōá compactly supported near zero. The first term converges by local continuity of u, and the second tends to zero using the random seminorm bound from (i) because the heat kernel tail is rapidly small in S(D). For (iii), apply the weak identity on each interval [(i‚ąí1)/n,i/n] with the frozen test function f_{(i‚ąí1)/n}, sum over i, and telescope. The deterministic time-derivative term is identified through ‚ąĎ‚ü®u_{i/n},f_{i/n}‚ąíf_{(i‚ąí1)/n}‚ü©; convergence follows from (i), continuity of f in the Schwartz topology, dominated convergence, and It√ī isometry for the noise term. The remaining sums define the nonlinear integral against H.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4: weak-type identity implies a mild-type identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.3, there exists a stochastic process R^H:[0,1]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR such that almost surely u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+R^H(t,x)+V_t(x) for all t‚ąą[0,1] and x‚ąąD. The process R^H is almost surely continuous on (0,1]√óD, and for every fixed t>0 and x‚ąąD, ‚ąę_0^{t‚ąíőĶ} H(dr,p_{t-r}(x,¬∑)) ‚Üí R^H(t,x) in probability as őĶ‚Üí0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First take a test function Ōē‚ąąS(D) and, for fixed terminal time t, insert f_s=P_{t-s}Ōē into Lemma 4.3(iii). Since ‚ąā_s f_s+1/2 őĒf_s=0, this gives ‚ü®u_t,Ōē‚ü©=‚ü®P_tu0,Ōē‚ü©+‚ąę_0^t H(dr,P_{t-r}Ōē)+‚ąę_0^t‚ąęP_{t-r}Ōē W. Next choose Ōē=p_őĶ(x,¬∑) and apply the identity at time t‚ąíőĶ. This yields P_őĶu_{t‚ąíőĶ}(x)=P_tu0(x)+‚ąę_0^{t‚ąíőĶ}H(dr,p_{t-r}(x,¬∑))+P_őĶV_{t‚ąíőĶ}(x). Lemma 4.3(ii) gives P_őĶu_{t‚ąíőĶ}(x)‚Üíu_t(x), while It√ī isometry gives P_őĶV_{t‚ąíőĶ}(x)‚ÜíV_t(x). Defining R^H(t,x)=u_t(x)‚ąíP_tu0(x)‚ąíV_t(x) proves both the mild identity and the convergence of the H-integral.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.5: identification of the mild drift obtained from a weak regularized solution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let őĪ>-3/2, b‚ąąC^őĪ(R), u0‚ąąB(D), and let u be a weak regularized solution with initial condition u0. Assume u‚ąąV(5/8). Let R^K be the process obtained from Lemma 4.4 by applying it to the weak regularized drift K. Then, for every sequence b_n‚ąąC‚ąě_b(R) with b_n‚Üíb in C^{őĪ‚ąí}, every t‚ąą[0,1], and every x‚ąąD, ‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b_n(u_r(y))dy dr ‚Üí R^K(t,x) in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a smooth f, t, x, and őĶ‚ąą(0,t). Decompose the difference between ‚ąę_0^t p_{t-r}f(u_r) and R^K(t,x) into three terms: I1 over [0,t‚ąíőĶ], comparing ‚ąęp f(u) with ‚ąęK(dr,p_{t-r}); I2 over the short terminal interval [t‚ąíőĶ,t]; and I3 comparing ‚ąę_0^{t‚ąíőĶ}K(dr,p_{t-r}) with R^K(t,x). Lemma 4.6 controls I1 in probability by ||f‚ąíb||_{C^{őĪ‚Ä≤}} for őĪ‚Ä≤‚ąą(-3/2,őĪ), using a time-discretization őļ_n, the weak regularized approximation property of K, and Proposition 3.4. Lemma 4.7 controls I2 by ||f||_{C^őĪ}őĶ^{1/2}. Lemma 4.4 gives I3‚Üí0 as őĶ‚Üí0. Taking f=b_n, first őĶ‚Üí0 and then n‚Üí‚ąě, and using b_n‚Üíb in C^{őĪ‚Ä≤}, gives the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.6: estimate comparing weak regularized drift and smooth drift before the terminal singularity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.5, fix őĪ‚Ä≤‚ąą(-3/2,őĪ). For f‚ąąC‚ąě_b(R), t‚ąą[0,1], x‚ąąD, őĶ‚ąą(0,t), define I_1^őĶ(t,x)=|‚ąę_0^{t‚ąíőĶ}‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)f(u_r(y))dy dr ‚ąí ‚ąę_0^{t‚ąíőĶ} K(dr,p_{t-r}(x,¬∑))|. Then there is C=C(őĪ,őĪ‚Ä≤) such that, for every őī>0, P(I_1^őĶ(t,x)>őī)‚Č§Cőī^{-1}(1+[u‚ąíV]_{C^{5/8}L^2([0,1])})||f‚ąíb||_{C^{őĪ‚Ä≤}}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof introduces the grid projection őļ_n(r)=‚Ćänr‚Ćč/n and splits I_1^őĶ into three errors. The first replaces p_{t-r} by p_{t‚ąíőļ_n(r)} and is bounded by Proposition 3.4 using the heat-kernel L^1 difference estimate ||p_{t-r}‚ąíp_{t‚ąíőļ_n(r)}||_{L^1}‚Č§Cn^{-1}őĶ^{-1/2}, hence vanishes as n‚Üí‚ąě. The second compares the frozen-kernel f-integral with the corresponding increments of K; by the weak regularized definition, K increments are limits of b_M-integrals, and Proposition 3.4 bounds the difference by ||f‚ąíb||_{C^{őĪ‚Ä≤}} plus a vanishing n^{-1/2} term. The third is the difference between the grid sum defining the nonlinear integral ‚ąęK(dr,p_{t-r}) and that integral itself; Lemma 4.3(iii) makes it vanish as n‚Üí‚ąě. Combining and letting n‚Üí‚ąě yields the probability bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.7: short-time terminal drift bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.5, there is C=C(őĪ) such that for every őī>0, every (s,t)‚ąąőĒ_{[0,1]}, every x‚ąąD, and every f‚ąąC‚ąě_b(R), P(|‚ąę_s^t‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)f(u_r(y))dy dr|>őī) ‚Č§ Cőī^{-1}||f||_{C^őĪ}(t-s)^{1/2}(1+[u‚ąíV]_{C^{5/8}L^2([0,1])}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Proposition 3.4 with Ōą=u‚ąíV, ŌĄ=5/8, X_r(y)=p_{t-r}(x,y), őĹ=0, and a negative regularity exponent controlled by the C^őĪ norm of f. Since ‚ąęp_{t-r}(x,y)dy=1 and őĪ>-3/2 gives the condition ő≥+4ŌĄ>1 at an admissible ő≥, the L^2 norm of the integral is bounded by C||f|| times (t-s)^{1/2}. Chebyshev's inequality gives the probability estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.8: weak identity obtained from a mild regularized solution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let őĪ>-3/2, b‚ąąC^őĪ(R), u0‚ąąB(D), and let u be a mild regularized solution with initial condition u0. Assume u‚ąąV(5/8). Then for every Ōē‚ąąS(D) and t‚ąą[0,1], there is a continuous process R_Ōē(t) such that ‚ąĎ_{i=1}^{‚Ćänt‚Ćč} ‚ü®K_{i/n}‚ąíP_{1/n}K_{(i‚ąí1)/n},Ōē‚ü© ‚Üí R_Ōē(t) in probability as n‚Üí‚ąě, and almost surely ‚ü®u_t,Ōē‚ü©=‚ü®u0,Ōē‚ü©+‚ąę_0^t ‚ü®u_s,1/2 őĒŌē‚ü©ds+R_Ōē(t)+‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D Ōē(x)W(ds,dx), for all t‚ąą[0,1].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define R_Ōē(t) directly by subtracting the initial, heat-Laplacian, and noise terms from ‚ü®u_t,Ōē‚ü©; Corollary 4.2 ensures the needed integrability and continuity. To identify R_Ōē as a limit of drift increments, use the mild decomposition u=P_tu0+K+V at grid times. The identity ‚ü®u_{i/n},Ōē‚ü©‚ąí‚ü®u_{(i‚ąí1)/n},P_{1/n}Ōē‚ü©=‚ü®K_{i/n}‚ąíP_{1/n}K_{(i‚ąí1)/n},Ōē‚ü©+‚ü®V_{i/n}‚ąíP_{1/n}V_{(i‚ąí1)/n},Ōē‚ü© is summed over i. The terms involving u and P_{1/n}Ōē‚ąíŌē converge to the Laplacian integral by local continuity plus weighted growth; the stochastic convolution telescoping sum converges to the stochastic integral by It√ī isometry. The remaining term is the desired limit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.9: identification of the weak drift obtained from a mild regularized solution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.8, for every sequence b_m‚ąąC‚ąě_b(R) with b_m‚Üíb in C^{őĪ‚ąí}, every Ōē‚ąąS(D), and every t‚ąą[0,1], ‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D Ōē(x)b_m(u_r(x))dx dr ‚Üí R_Ōē(t) in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof compares ‚ąęŌē b_m(u) with the grid drift-increment sum from Lemma 4.8. The difference is split into four terms: replacing Ōē by a heat-evolved kernel average over each grid interval; comparing the resulting expression with the K increments using the mild regularized approximation property; the difference between the K-increment sum and R_Ōē(t); and the final short interval [őļ_n(t),t]. Proposition 3.4 controls the first and last terms by powers of n^{-1}. For the K-comparison term, the definition of mild regularized solution expresses K as a limit of b_M-integrals, and Proposition 3.4 bounds the resulting b_M‚ąíb_m difference by ||b_M‚ąíb_m||_{C^{őĪ‚Ä≤}} for őĪ‚Ä≤<őĪ. Letting M‚Üí‚ąě yields a bound involving ||b‚ąíb_m||_{C^{őĪ‚Ä≤}}. Finally take m‚Üí‚ąě and n‚Üí‚ąě, using Lemma 4.8 for the grid-sum convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.10: classical mild-a.e. solutions with Lp drift belong to V(1‚ąí1/(4p))
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let p‚ąą[1,‚ąě], b‚ąąL^p(R), u0‚ąąB(D), and let u:(0,1]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be jointly continuous and adapted. Suppose that for every t‚ąą[0,1], for Lebesgue-a.e. x‚ąąD, ‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr<‚ąě almost surely and u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b(u_r(y))dy dr+V_t(x) almost surely. Then u‚ąąV(1‚ąí1/(4p)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the full-measure set D_t of spatial points where the mild identity holds, Lemma 4.11 gives ||‚ąę_s^t‚ąęp_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr||_{L^m}‚Č§C|t-s|^{1‚ąí1/(4p)} for x‚ąąD_t. Therefore for x‚ąąD_t, the semigroup increment u_t‚ąíV_t‚ąíP_{t-s}(u_s‚ąíV_s) equals the drift integral over [s,t] and satisfies the same bound. Since u and V are continuous in x, Fatou's lemma extends the estimate from x‚ąąD_t to all x‚ąąD. This is exactly the defining V(1‚ąí1/(4p)) estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.11: Lp drift-increment estimate via random controls
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.10, let D_T be the full-measure set of spatial points x for which the assumed mild integrability and identity hold at time T. Then for every m‚Č•2, every (s,T)‚ąąőĒ_{[0,1]}, and every x‚ąąD_T, ||‚ąę_s^T‚ąę_D p_{T-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr||_{L^m} ‚Č§ C|T-s|^{1‚ąí1/(4p)}, where C depends on m, p, and ||b||_{L^p(R)} but not on s,T,x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For p<‚ąě, apply Lemma 3.3 with ő≥=‚ąí1/p, f=|b|, Ōą=u‚ąíV, and w_{s,t}(x)=1_{x‚ąąD_t}‚ąę_s^t‚ąęp_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr. The mild identity verifies |Ōą_t‚ąíP_{t-s}Ōą_s|‚Č§w_{s,t}, and the semigroup property gives P_{t-u}w_{s,u}+w_{u,t}=w_{s,t}. The embedding L^p‚äāC^{‚ąí1/p} gives the needed C^ő≥ norm. Lemma 3.3 yields an inequality in which the desired quantity is bounded by C||b||_{L^p}(t-s)^{3/4‚ąí1/(4p)} times itself plus a remainder of order |t-s|^{1‚ąí1/(4p)}. On sufficiently short intervals, the self-term is absorbed; longer intervals are handled by partitioning into short subintervals. For p=‚ąě, the paper takes M=||b||‚ąě and observes that the part |b|1_{|b|>M} vanishes a.e.; the remaining bounded part gives the linear bound M|T-s|, which is stronger.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.12: analytically weak solutions tested against time-dependent functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let b:R‚ÜíR be measurable, u0‚ąąB(D), and let u be an analytically weak solution. Let f‚ąąC([0,1],S(D)) satisfy sup_{t‚ąą[0,1]} sup_{x‚ąąD} |f_t(x)|e^{|x|}<‚ąě. Then for every t‚ąą[0,1], almost surely ‚ü®u_t,f_t‚ü©=‚ü®u0,f0‚ü©+‚ąę_0^t ‚ü®u_r,‚ąā_r f_r+1/2 őĒf_r‚ü©dr+‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D b(u_r(x))f_r(x)dx dr+‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D f_r(x)W(dr,dx). Moreover, if Ōē‚ąąS(D) and sup_x |Ōē(x)|e^{|x|}<‚ąě, then for every t‚ąą[0,1], almost surely ‚ü®u_t,Ōē‚ü©=‚ü®u0,P_tŌē‚ü©+‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D P_{t-r}Ōē(x)b(u_r(x))dx dr+‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D P_{t-r}Ōē(x)W(dr,dx).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First, the analytically weak definition implies ‚ąę_0^1‚ąę_D |b(u_r(y))|e^{‚ąí|y|}dy dr<‚ąě almost surely, because one can choose a nonnegative test function dominating e^{‚ąí|x|}. Apply Lemma 4.3 with H_t(Ōē)=‚ąę_0^t‚ąęb(u_r(y))Ōē(y)dy dr. The Riemann sums defining ‚ąęH(ds,f_s) are ‚ąę_0^{őļ_n(t)}‚ąęb(u_r(y))f_{őļ_n(r)}(y)dy dr, which converge by dominated convergence to ‚ąę_0^t‚ąęb(u_r(y))f_r(y)dy dr, using the exponential decay assumption. This identifies the H-integral in Lemma 4.3(iii) and gives the time-dependent weak identity. Taking f_r=P_{t-r}Ōē makes ‚ąā_r f_r+1/2 őĒf_r=0 and yields the heat-evolved identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.13: analytically weak solutions satisfy the mild identity for a.e. spatial point
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.12, for every t‚ąą[0,1], for Lebesgue-a.e. x‚ąąD one has ‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr<‚ąě almost surely and u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b(u_r(y))dy dr+V_t(x) almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The integrability follows by Fubini from ‚ąę|b(u)|e^{‚ąí|y|}<‚ąě and the elementary inequality ‚ąí|x|‚Č§|x‚ąíy|‚ąí|y|, which controls the heat-kernel convolution weighted by e^{‚ąí|x|}. To identify the mild identity, apply the heat-evolved identity of Lemma 4.12 at time t‚ąíőĶ with the test function Ōē=p_őĶ(x,¬∑). This gives P_őĶu_{t‚ąíőĶ}(x)=P_tu0(x)+‚ąę_0^{t‚ąíőĶ}‚ąęp_{t-r}(x,y)b(u_r(y))dy dr+P_őĶV_{t‚ąíőĶ}(x). Lemma 4.3(ii) gives P_őĶu_{t‚ąíőĶ}(x)‚Üíu_t(x), It√ī isometry gives P_őĶV_{t‚ąíőĶ}(x)‚ÜíV_t(x), and for the a.e. x where the absolute drift integral is finite the drift integral converges by ordinary dominated convergence as őĶ‚Üí0.

proof_techniques.name: Regularized solution via universal smooth drift approximation
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines solutions with distributional drift by requiring convergence against every smooth bounded approximating sequence b_n‚Üíb in the appropriate Besov topology. In the mild version, the convolutions ‚ąęp_{t-r}b_n(u_r) converge locally uniformly in probability to a process K_t(x). In the weak version, the test-function integrals ‚ąęŌē b_n(u_r) converge uniformly in time in probability to K_t(Ōē). This makes the distributional term b(u) meaningful without assigning pointwise values to b(u_t(x)).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Dirac drift in the original problem, this is the formal mechanism that supports replacing őī0 by heat-kernel mollifiers G_{1/n} and interpreting the limiting drift as a canonical additive term. Under the problem's assumed Dirichlet extension, it justifies using approximation limits when reasoning about the Markov semigroup.

proof_techniques.name: Stochastic sewing with random controls
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly controls singular nonlinear integrals by comparing f(V_r+Ōą_r) with f(V_r+P_{r-s}Ōą_s), where V is the stochastic convolution and Ōą is a perturbation with semigroup increments controlled by a random superadditive quantity w. Conditional expectations are estimated after Gaussian smoothing by the future part of the stochastic convolution, while martingale errors are handled in L^m. Proposition 3.2 then sews local estimates into global integral bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique is relevant for controlling occupation of the solution near the singular level 0, which is exactly where a őī0 drift concentrates. It may help establish tightness or uniform estimates for approximating equations with drifts G_{1/n}.

proof_techniques.name: Besov regularization by the stochastic convolution
proof_techniques.description: The estimates exploit that, conditionally on the past, increments of the stochastic convolution introduce Gaussian smoothing in the scalar variable. This converts a negative Besov norm ||f||_{C^ő≥}, ő≥<0, into usable bounds for integrals of f(V+Ōą), with time exponents such as 1+ő≥/4. The condition ő≥+4ŌĄ>1 balances the singularity of f against the semigroup H√∂lder control of Ōą.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A Dirac mass is a negative-regularity distribution, so this is the analytical mechanism behind making ‚ąęőī0(u) meaningful. It suggests looking for estimates that depend on the C^ő≥ norm of the approximating drifts rather than on their sup norms, which blow up for G_{1/n}.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical drift limits independent of the approximation
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.1 constructs limits H^f for drift integrals whenever u‚ąąV(ŌĄ) and f‚ąąC^ő≥ with ő≥+4ŌĄ>1. The proof first proves uniform moment H√∂lder bounds and Cauchy estimates for the approximating integral processes, then applies a Kolmogorov convergence criterion to obtain a jointly continuous limit. Interlacing two approximating sequences proves that the limit is intrinsic and does not depend on the chosen mollifier.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If two invariant-measure arguments use different smooth approximations to őī0, this result is the kind of tool that prevents approximation-dependence. In the original setting, the assumed convergence u^n(t)‚Üíu(t) can be paired with such intrinsic-limit ideas.

proof_techniques.name: Weak-to-mild conversion by time-dependent heat tests
proof_techniques.description: The paper converts a weak identity into a mild one by testing against f_s=P_{t-s}Ōē, for which ‚ąā_s f_s+1/2 őĒf_s=0. To justify time-dependent tests, it proves a partition/telescoping formula and defines ‚ąęH(ds,f_s) as a limit of Riemann sums. It then takes Ōē=p_őĶ(x,¬∑) and lets őĶ‚Üď0, using a Mitoma-type distributional continuity bound and It√ī isometry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is directly useful if the Dirichlet solution is defined weakly or probabilistically but an invariant-measure proof needs the mild heat-kernel representation. The original problem explicitly assumes a probabilistically weak PDE mild solution; this technique explains how weak and mild formulations can be reconciled.

proof_techniques.name: Mild-to-weak conversion by grid telescoping
proof_techniques.description: Starting from u=P_tu0+K_t+V_t, the paper compares ‚ü®u_{i/n},Ōē‚ü© and ‚ü®u_{(i‚ąí1)/n},P_{1/n}Ōē‚ü© over a time grid. The heat semigroup cancels the initial condition, the stochastic convolution increments converge to the It√ī integral, and the K increments define the weak drift. A second approximation argument identifies this weak drift with the limit of ‚ąęŌē b_n(u).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an argument establishes properties of the mild Dirichlet solution but needs martingale-problem or weak-form identities, this telescoping method is the portable bridge, assuming analogous Dirichlet heat-kernel estimates.

proof_techniques.name: Automatic V-regularity for Lp drifts
proof_techniques.description: For integrable drifts, the paper proves that any analytically weak or mild solution automatically belongs to V(1‚ąí1/(4p)). The proof uses the drift integral itself as the random control w in Lemma 3.3, absorbs a small multiple of the desired quantity on short intervals, and patches short intervals to obtain a global estimate. This removes the need to assume V-regularity separately in the Lp case.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although őī0 is not an Lp function, the absorption strategy suggests how one might prove V-type bounds for measure drifts by using occupation/local-time controls. Such bounds are often prerequisites for approximation and uniqueness arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Approximation identification through negative Besov norms
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 3.5 shows that for h‚ąąLp and bounded smooth g, the integral of (h‚ąíg)(u) against a kernel X is controlled by ||h‚ąíg||_{C^ő≥}, ő≥‚ąą(-3/2,-1), times a V-type norm of u and an L1 norm of X. This lets the paper identify regularized drift limits with classical Lebesgue drift integrals when b‚ąąLp.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For invariant measures obtained as limits from smooth approximating drifts, analogous estimates could be used to pass semigroup or occupation identities to the singular limit. For őī0, one would need the corresponding distributional version rather than the Lp tail argument.

other_useful_info: The paper's main theorems are stated only for R, periodic [0,1], and Neumann [0,1] settings; Dirichlet boundary conditions are not part of the formal results, so the original problem's assumption that ABLM-type results extend to Dirichlet is essential for direct use. The paper cites Athreya-Butkovsky-L√™-Mytnik [1] for existence and uniqueness of mild regularized solutions in certain Besov regimes and specifically invokes [1, Theorem 2.6] for strong probabilistic existence and uniqueness in V(3/4) in the proof of Corollary 2.12. The introduction also states that weak probabilistic existence and uniqueness of mild regularized solutions for b‚ąąC^őĪ, őĪ>-3/2, was obtained in a very recent cited work [5]. The paper contains no invariant-measure, strong Feller, irreducibility, coupling, or ergodicity theorem; its direct contribution to the original problem is solution-notion compatibility and approximation/occupation estimates for singular drifts.
why_relevant: This recent paper clarifies weak and mild solution notions for stochastic heat equations with irregular/distributional drift. It is useful for checking that the Markov semigroup and approximation limits used in invariant-measure arguments are compatible with the ABLM solution concept.
